steps.title: Identify the trade-off
steps.content: Hawthorne compares historical writing to a map: accurate and useful but cold and lacking the charm of a landscape painting. He then says these defects could be partly fixed, but not by methods allowed to strict biographical exactness.

steps.title: Reject unsupported ideas
steps.content: Choice A wrongly suggests historians should not care about seeming inaccurate. Choice C invents disagreement among historians. Choice D treats the map and painting as literal objects instead of an analogy. The main idea is that strict truthfulness can make historical writing less engaging. The answer is B.
